name: Blue Bottle Coffee
description: Craft-coffee reference — signature deep navy on warm bone-cream, editorial restraint, photography-led storytelling. The brand's confidence comes from absence: enormous whitespace, one accent, never more.

--color-bottle-anchor.role: Signature surface and text — deep navy that names the brand
--color-bottle-anchor.context: the Blue Bottle blue, used as primary text and hero anchor

--color-bone-cream.role: Primary surface — page background, product card fills

--color-paper-base.role: Pure-white surface for product photography backdrops
--color-paper-base.context: studio white, used only behind product imagery

--color-graphite-body.role: Secondary body text on cream — desaturated cousin of bottle-anchor

--color-amber-accent.role: Optional accent — single-origin tasting notes, seasonal coffee callouts
--color-amber-accent.context: warm amber, used at most once per page

--color-fog-divider.role: Hairline dividers, low-emphasis borders, product card outlines
--color-fog-divider.context: cream-shadow divider that disappears at scale

--font-display-serif.role: Display headlines — origin stories, hero, editorial titling

--font-body-grotesk.role: Body copy, product names, navigation, tasting notes

antiPatterns: Never use rounded corners on hero or product photography — Blue Bottle's photographic restraint requires sharp edges. Rounded photo treatments break the editorial character.
Never apply --color-amber-accent to UI chrome (buttons, links) — it earns its presence by appearing only in tasting-notes-storytelling moments. As a CTA color it breaks the navy-monopoly rule.
Whitespace must dominate — sections with content density above ~40% viewport coverage stop reading as Blue Bottle. The brand IS the breathing room.
Body copy must never use --font-display-serif — the serif lives at display sizes only. Serif body type reads as legacy magazine, not specialty coffee.
